spring: Spring peepers start singing in the wet meadows by mid-April — listen at dusk.
Trout lilies bloom before the canopy leafs out. Look in the woodsy margins off the old timber roads.
Mud season: stick to gravel paths so the trails don't rut.
Morel mushrooms fruit in the bottomlands when redbuds bloom — usually late April.
Watch for migrating warblers refueling in the hedgerows during the first two weeks of May.
summer: Fireflies peak in early July — the tall-grass strips along the creek bottoms are reliable.
Wild blackberries ripen along the rail trail by late July. Bring a container.
Chiggers love the tall grass by July — tuck your socks in.
Listen for the dickcissel buzzing its name from the fence posts in the unmowed fields.
Smithville Lake herons roost in the big sycamores on the north cove — best seen at dusk.
fall: Oak and hickory peak in late October — the bluffs along the creek go gold and rust.
Watch for monarchs funneling south through the prairie in September.
First frost usually lands by mid-October in Plattsburg. Pull the basil before then.
Pawpaws ripen in the bottomland woods in September — sweet and worth finding.
November 15 to March 15 is the best time to prune most trees and shrubs.
winter: Bald eagles gather below Smithville Dam through January — bring binoculars.
Track prints in new snow: fox tracks run in a near-straight line.
Thin ice in the lake coves even when the middle looks solid. Stay on the bank.
Winter sun and wind can damage young trees — protect bark from deer and rodents.
Cedar waxwings strip the hackberry trees bare by February. Watch the hedgerows.
